explanations:
<thought>I should explain a general predictive rule without citing any collected measurements, since the user cannot run experiments and I must not provide observations. A simple cubic-height model anchored to a familiar reference will be easiest to use.</thought>
<answer>To predict an adult human’s weight from height alone, use the idea that body mass grows roughly with volume, and volume scales like height³.

A simple rule is:

Weight (kg) ≈ 70 × (height in cm / 170)³

Why this works:
- If two adults have similar body proportions and density, the taller one is larger in all three dimensions.
- So weight increases approximately with the cube of height, not linearly.

How to use it:
1. Take height in cm.
2. Divide by 170.
3. Cube the result.
4. Multiply by 70.

Examples of the method:
- 160 cm: 70 × (160/170)³
- 180 cm: 70 × (180/170)³

This gives a reasonable estimate for an average adult. It will not be exact for every person, because real weight also depends on sex, muscle mass, fat level, frame size, and age.

Useful shortcut:
Weight ≈ k × height³, with k ≈ 1.4×10⁻⁵ if height is in cm.

So for quick prediction, the 170 cm, 70 kg scaled-cube formula is the easiest practical rule.</answer>

scientist_messages:
role:system, messaage:Adult humans of varying heights have weights that scale roughly with the cube of their height, modulated by a near-constant body density.
Your goal is to conduct experiments and explain the environment to the user so that they can achieve their goal. The goal of the user is to be able to predict the weight (kg) of an adult human given their height (cm).
Make observations by specifying a single height (cm) you want to observe with a real number. The environment will return the weight of an adult human at that height (kg).
The height values are between 150.0 and 190.0.
You may also think before providing your predictions.

Here is an example:
<thought> your thought </thought>
<observe>170</observe>
